narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because a model flagged a 43% chance of not passing the bar exam feels deeply unfair, especially when you've poured so much into getting here. Picture the model as an experienced advisor who's seen thousands of students like you navigate this path, and it pieced together your story from the details you shared. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 4.0, placing you in the lower half of your class by then—this was the biggest signal pulling toward struggle on the bar, as if the tougher years revealed challenges that might carry over. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 8.0, a strong start that faded, painting a picture of momentum lost over time. Your middle family income added another layer, suggesting fewer resources to buffer the intense grind ahead compared to peers with more support. As an Asian male, those demographics also nudged the outlook toward difficulty, based on patterns the model has observed in similar journeys. Your LSAT score of 21.5 and undergrad GPA of 2.9 reinforced this, showing solid but not standout academic firepower for the bar's demands. Even going full-time, which typically helps focus, didn't fully counterbalance these in the model's view. The one bright spot was your gender, which slightly tipped things toward success in past cases. Together, these factors created a narrative of early promise dimming under pressure, leading the school to play it safe with spots that are so precious. But here's the empowering part, Alex: this isn't your whole story—your target law passion shines through, and with targeted steps like bolstering those later grades or seeking extra prep, you could rewrite the ending. You've got the drive; now it's about building the momentum that matches it. Keep pushing—you're closer than this one prediction suggests.
